questions.question: A bottle contains water (density $1000\text{ kg/m}^3$) and another identical bottle contains oil (density $800\text{ kg/m}^3$). How does the pressure at the bottom of the oil bottle compare to the water bottle?
options: The pressure at the bottom of the oil is less
The pressure at the bottom of the oil is greater
The pressures are equal because the bottles are identical
The oil bottle has zero pressure at the bottom
questions.explanation:
Since $Delta p = rho g h$, pressure is directly proportional to density. Oil is less dense than water, so it exerts less pressure at the same depth. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking that identical volumes or containers result in equal pressures regardless of the liquid type.

questions.question: Calculate the pressure exerted by water at a depth of $5.0\text{ m}$. (Take density of water $= 1000\text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation:
$Delta p = rho g h = 1000 \times 10 \times 5.0 = 50000\text{ Pa}$. 

**Common mistake:** Forgetting to include the factor $g=10$, leading to an answer of $5000$.

questions.question: A marine biologist experiences a change in pressure of $51250\text{ Pa}$ while diving. If the density of seawater is $1025\text{ kg/m}^3$, what is her depth? (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation:
$Delta h = Delta p / (rho g) = 51250 / (1025 \times 10) = 51250 / 10250 = 5.0\text{ m}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying the pressure by density instead of dividing.

questions.question: A container is filled with a liquid of density $880\text{ kg/m}^3$. What is the change in pressure when an object is lowered from point A (depth $0.08\text{ m}$) to point B (depth $0.25\text{ m}$)?
questions.explanation:
$Delta h = 0.25 - 0.08 = 0.17\text{ m}$. $Delta p = 880 \times 10 \times 0.17 = 1496\text{ Pa}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using only the depth at point B ($0.25$) rather than the difference between A and B.

questions.question: Which graph correctly shows how the pressure $p$ in a liquid of constant density varies with depth $h$?
options: A straight line passing through the origin
A curve that gets steeper as depth increases
A straight line with a negative gradient
questions.explanation:
Because $p$ is directly proportional to $h$ ($p = rho g h$), the relationship is linear and the graph is a straight line through the origin. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the graph should be a curve because liquid pressure feels 'heavy'.
